file_name: Krishnan et al. - 2026 - IFLCNND intelligent federated learning with deep convolutional neural network model for classifying.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) within a federated learning (FL) framework (IFLCNND) to classify nine dermatological conditions (including melanoma, nevus, and birthmark) from over 20,000 annotated skin images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a custom federated learning model (IFLCNND) using a CNN on over 20,000 dermatological images. It includes specific methodology (manual FL simulation, downsampling), results (accuracy metrics, confusion matrices), and new findings, distinguishing it from a review paper despite containing a literature survey section.

title: IFLCNND: intelligent federated learning with deep convolutional neural network model for classifying dermatology
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying nine dermatological conditions, including melanoma and nevus, using a custom intelligent federated learning framework named IFLCNND. The researchers utilized a dataset of over 20,000 annotated skin images to train a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) within a manually simulated federated learning environment to handle non-IID data distributions. Initial attempts using the Flower framework with standard FedAvg were replaced by a custom simulation to mitigate communication issues and data heterogeneity. The model's performance was evaluated using a validation set of 288 images, assessing metrics such as precision, recall, F1-scores, and confusion matrices. The IFLCNND model achieved approximately 96% validation accuracy (macro-averaged F1-score) and demonstrated state-of-the-art class-wise accuracy for key classes like Melanoma, Nevus, and Birthmark. The results indicate that this lightweight, privacy-preserving federated approach offers a robust and scalable alternative for dermatological diagnosis without requiring complex infrastructure.

all_task_types.target: Nine dermatological conditions (AKIEC, BCC, Birthmark, DF, Eczema, Melanoma, Nevus, Psoriasis, Vitiligo)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin diseases using a CNN within a federated learning framework. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using precision, recall, F1-scores, and confusion matrices on a validation set of 288 images. The primary metric reported is approximately 96% validation accuracy (macro-averaged F1-score).

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Dermatology Dataset (Combined)
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive public dataset obtained from Kaggle's public domain, combining multiple sources including the HAM10000 dataset and a dedicated Kaggle Melanoma dataset. It contains annotated skin images covering nine distinct dermatological conditions.

use.training: Partitioned into three simulated clients with non-IID distributions for federated learning training. Client 1 received ~9,506 images (Melanoma, BCC, AKIEC, DF, Nevus); Client 2 and Client 3 received remaining subsets. Data was sampled proportionally during local training.
use.validation: Used for evaluating model performance (precision, recall, F1-scores, confusion matrices) on a subset of 288 images.
use.other: Used to simulate realistic clinical heterogeneity by partitioning data across three clients. Also used for a separate experiment on Client 3 (Eczema and Psoriasis) to assess image enhancement effects.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model reliability, true positive rates, and classification performance across nine dermatological classes on a validation set of 288 images.
To assess classification performance by class, providing metrics for precision, recall, and F1-scores (macro-averaged and weighted-averaged) to account for class imbalance.
To analyze performance by class and ensure global accuracy was not influenced by the large number of examples in certain categories.
To provide derived scores for each class to further assess classification performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores, and confusion matrices.

Kaggle Dermatology Dataset (Combined).notes: The final model achieved 96% validation accuracy. Class-wise accuracy for AKIEC appeared high (~99%) due to class imbalance (8 samples in test set, 280 non-AKIEC samples). The down-sampled configuration demonstrated better fairness and stability across heterogeneous data distributions compared to the full configuration which showed slight overfitting.

Client 3 Dataset (Eczema and Psoriasis).notes: Image enhancement (contrast and sharpening) reduced accuracy, likely due to amplified noise and edge distortions interfering with CNN feature extraction in texture-heavy medical images.

architecture_used.main_model: IFLCNND (Intelligent Federated Learning with Deep Convolutional Neural Network)

training_details.framework: Manually simulated federated learning environment (Python, TensorFlow, Google Colab)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: IFLCNND (Intelligent Federated Learning with Deep Convolutional Neural Network)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to uniform resolution of 180 × 180 pixels
Cropping to be lesion-centric to limit background artifacts
Down-sampling (limiting each class to 1,000 images per client)
Proportional sampling of datasets during local client model training to ensure equal class updates
Image enhancement (contrast and sharpening) - tested but found to reduce accuracy due to amplified noise
conclusions: The proposed IFLCNND framework, utilizing a manually simulated federated learning approach with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), achieved approximately 96% validation accuracy and a macro-averaged F1-score of 0.96 on a dataset of nine dermatological conditions.

The model demonstrated state-of-the-art class-wise accuracy for key classes including Melanoma, Nevus, and Birthmark, indicating strong generalization and robustness in a privacy-preserving, decentralized environment.

A custom, manually simulated federated learning environment was found to be more effective than the Flower framework with standard FedAvg for handling non-IID data distributions and communication issues typical in clinical datasets.

Down-sampling class sizes to a maximum of 1,000 images per class per client improved fairness, stability, and generalization by mitigating overfitting and bias toward dominant classes, without degrading diagnostic accuracy.

Image enhancement techniques (contrast and sharpening) reduced model performance on specific classes (Eczema and Psoriasis) by amplifying noise and edge distortions, suggesting that such preprocessing may be detrimental for texture-heavy medical images in this context.

The study highlights that manual FL simulation offers a scalable, easy-to-deploy, and privacy-preserving alternative for real-world medical applications, requiring no complex infrastructure while effectively adhering to data privacy principles.
important_remarks: The study relies on a manually simulated federated learning environment rather than a real-world multi-institutional deployment, limiting the assessment of true clinical heterogeneity and communication constraints.

The dataset is heavily imbalanced (e.g., 6,662 Birthmarks vs. 191 Dermatofibroma), and the initial high accuracy for minority classes like AKIEC (~99%) was driven by true negatives in a small support set (n=8) rather than robust feature learning.
The validation set is very small (288 images), which may lead to unstable performance estimates and lacks statistical power for reliable class-wise comparisons.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between configurations.

Data augmentation was explicitly avoided because initial tests showed it reduced model accuracy, suggesting the preprocessing pipeline (cropping, normalization) was deemed sufficient but potentially limiting generalizability.

Image enhancement techniques (contrast and sharpening) negatively impacted performance on noisy classes like Eczema and Psoriasis, indicating that standard preprocessing may be detrimental for texture-heavy medical images.

The model was evaluated on a single public dataset (Kaggle/HAM10000), raising concerns about external validation and potential bias towards specific imaging protocols or demographics present in that source.

The 'state-of-the-art' claim for class-wise accuracy is based on a specific down-sampled configuration designed to mitigate imbalance, which may not reflect performance on the original, unbalanced clinical data distribution.
